serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Matcha Cheese Cloud Dessert is approximately 150 g, equivalent to one cup or individual portion as typically served at HeyTea and similar tea-house dessert outlets.

nutrient_source.title: Cheesecake (cream cheese-based dessert) and matcha powder composite estimate; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Cream cheese (FDC ID 1017822), Whipping cream (FDC ID 1053608), Matcha powder (FDC ID 2003571), Sugar (FDC ID 1705030)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Matcha Cheese Cloud Dessert exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of the primary ingredients: cream cheese (~40%), whipping cream (~25%), milk (~15%), matcha powder (~5%), sugar (~14%), salt (~1%). Proportions are based on standard HeyTea-style cheese cloud dessert formulations documented in food science literature. Values are per 100 g of the assembled dessert.

gi_source.title: Cheesecake GI analogous estimate — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Matcha Cheese Cloud Dessert. GI estimated at 43 (low) based on the closest analogous food: cheesecake (GI ~43, University of Sydney GI Database). The dessert shares a similar macronutrient profile — high fat and moderate sugar from dairy — which suppresses glycemic response. Matcha polyphenols (EGCG) may further attenuate postprandial glucose, supporting a low GI classification. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as: (43 × 18.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 1.5) / 100 = 11.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~145 mg/100 g) and saturated fat load from cream cheese and whipping cream
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one small serving (≤100 g) and avoid frequent consumption, as the combined sodium and saturated fat content may contribute to elevated blood pressure over time.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate sugar content (~15.6 g/100 g) and added sugars from cream cheese base, partially offset by low GI (~43) and fat-mediated glycemic attenuation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may consume this dessert occasionally in small portions (≤100 g), as its low GI (~43) and high fat content slow glucose absorption, but total carbohydrate and sugar load per serving should be counted within the daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat content (~11.8 g/100 g) and cholesterol (~62 mg/100 g) from cream cheese and whipping cream
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this dessert as an occasional indulgence only, as its high saturated fat and cholesterol content can adversely affect LDL cholesterol levels and overall cardiovascular risk when consumed regularly.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Matcha Cheese Cloud Dessert is a chilled, layered dessert originating from Chinese tea-house culture and popularised in Singapore and Southeast Asia by brands such as HeyTea. It consists of a base of fine Japanese matcha (green tea powder) topped with a light, airy 'cheese cloud' — a whipped mixture of cream cheese, whipping cream, milk, sugar, and a pinch of salt. The cheese cloud topping has a mousse-like texture: simultaneously rich, creamy, and delicate, with a subtle savoury-sweet contrast that balances the earthy bitterness of matcha. The dessert is served chilled and is typically presented in a cup or small bowl. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense (~248 kcal/100 g) due to its high fat content from dairy (total fat ~18.5 g/100 g, saturated fat ~11.8 g/100 g). Sugar content is moderate (~15.6 g/100 g). Its glycemic index is estimated at approximately 43 (low), as the high fat and protein content of the dairy base slows gastric emptying and glucose absorption. Matcha polyphenols, particularly epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG), may provide additional glycemic attenuation. Clinically, this dessert is of moderate concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its sugar and carbohydrate load, and of high concern for those with cardiovascular disease due to its saturated fat and cholesterol content. It contains dairy allergens and is not suitable for vegans or those who are lactose intolerant. It is vegetarian-friendly. The product is sold at HeyTea outlets in Singapore, including Marina Bay Sands, and is listed on field survey data.
common_user_queries: How many calories in HeyTea Matcha Cloud?
Is Matcha Cloud dessert suitable for diabetics?
What is the GI of matcha cheese dessert?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Matcha Cheese Cloud Dessert exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of primary ingredients using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data. GI is estimated by analogy to cheesecake from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy ingredient data (cream cheese, whipping cream, milk, matcha powder, sugar, salt) weighted by approximate formulation proportions. No direct laboratory analysis or approved-source entry exists for this specific product. 2. GI of 43 is an analogous estimate based on cheesecake (University of Sydney GI Database); actual GI may vary depending on exact sugar content and formulation. 3. Serving size of 150 g is an estimate based on typical HeyTea portion sizes; actual weight may vary by outlet. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from ingredient composites and are not directly reported by a single approved source for this product; they are provided as best-estimate values. 5. Halal status is listed as false because HeyTea Singapore does not carry a Singapore MUIS halal certification as of the knowledge cutoff; consumers should verify current certification status. 6. This product contains dairy and is not suitable for individuals with lactose intolerance or dairy allergies. 7. The cuisine category is classified as 'Chinese Tea House / Dessert' reflecting the brand origin; multilingual naming follows the Singaporean dishes rule (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the product is served in Singapore. 8. No -1 sentinel values are used for core macros; all secondary nutrient -1 values in the template have been replaced with estimated numeric values derived from ingredient composites, so no -1 sentinel documentation is required.
